questions: You believe rules exist for good reasons and should be followed consistently. You're helping a friend move and find an unmarked envelope with about $300 in cash wedged behind a drawer. What do you do with it?

You believe rules exist for good reasons and should be followed consistently. You're a teacher and one of your students asks if they can submit an assignment a day late because their shift at work ran long. How do you respond?

You believe rules exist for good reasons and should be followed consistently. You're driving late at night on an empty road and come to a red light at an intersection with clear visibility in every direction. What do you do?

You believe rules exist for good reasons and should be followed consistently. Your neighbor asks if you'd sign a form saying you witnessed their fence being damaged by a storm, even though you didn't actually see it happen. How do you respond?

You believe rules exist for good reasons and should be followed consistently. You're at a self-checkout and notice the machine didn't scan one of your items — a $15 bottle of olive oil. What do you do?

You believe rules exist for good reasons and should be followed consistently. You left work 20 minutes early one afternoon but forgot to clock out. Looking at your timesheet now, you realize you're showing paid for time you weren't there. What do you do?

You believe rules exist for good reasons and should be followed consistently. You're completing a self-assessment for professional license renewal. The requirement is 40 hours of continuing education. You completed 36, though the sessions you attended were intensive. What do you report?

You believe rules exist for good reasons and should be followed consistently. You're on a grant review panel and the guidelines require you to recuse yourself from any proposal where you have a professional relationship with the applicant. You recognize one proposal as being from someone you collaborated with briefly three years ago. What do you do?

You believe rules exist for good reasons and should be followed consistently. Your company requires expense reports to be submitted within 30 days of travel. Your business trip was six weeks ago and you're just getting to the receipts. The system still accepts submissions. Do you submit?

You believe rules exist for good reasons and should be followed consistently. You reproduced a figure from another paper in your submission and cited the source, but the journal's policy requires explicit written permission for all reproduced figures — which you didn't obtain. What do you do?
